life-cycles.title: Life Cycles of Animals
keyQuestions: What is metamorphosis?
What is the difference between complete and incomplete metamorphosis?
Name 3 animals with complete metamorphosis and 3 with incomplete metamorphosis.
What stage comes after the egg in a butterfly's life cycle?
Why do some animals NOT have a metamorphosis stage?
keyIdeas: Every living thing goes through a life cycle: birth, growth, reproduction, and death. The cycle continues because offspring reproduce in turn.
Some animals undergo complete metamorphosis (butterfly, frog, mosquito, beetle). Their life cycle has four stages: egg, larva, pupa, adult. The young looks very different from the adult.
Some animals undergo incomplete metamorphosis (grasshopper, cockroach, dragonfly). Their life cycle has three stages: egg, nymph, adult. The nymph looks similar to the adult.

glossary.definition: The series of changes an organism goes through from birth to death. Includes all stages of growth, reproduction, and development.
glossary.template: The series of stages a living thing goes through from ___ to ___ to ___.

glossary.definition: A process of dramatic physical change in the body form of an organism as it develops from juvenile to adult. Can be complete or incomplete.
A life cycle with FOUR distinct stages: egg → larva → pupa → adult. The organism looks completely different at each stage. Examples: butterfly, mosquito, housefly, beetle.
glossary.template: A life cycle with ___ stages, where the young looks ___ from the adult.

glossary.definition: A life cycle with THREE stages: egg → nymph → adult. The nymph resembles a smaller, wingless version of the adult. Examples: grasshopper, cockroach, dragonfly.
glossary.template: A life cycle with ___ stages, where the young looks ___ to the adult.

glossary.definition: The second stage in complete metamorphosis. Often looks very different from the adult — e.g. caterpillar (butterfly), maggot (housefly), grub (beetle).
The third stage in complete metamorphosis. The organism is enclosed in a casing (chrysalis for butterflies, cocoon for moths) and undergoes internal reorganisation.
The juvenile stage in incomplete metamorphosis. Resembles a small adult but lacks wings. Grows through a series of moults.
The process of shedding the outer skin (exoskeleton) to grow larger. Nymphs moult several times before becoming adults.
The aquatic larval stage of a frog or toad. Has a tail and gills; breathes underwater. Gradually develops legs and lungs as it transforms into a froglet.
An intermediate stage between tadpole and adult frog. Has both a tail (shrinking) and legs, and starts breathing air.
To produce offspring. Life cycles exist so organisms can reproduce and continue their species.

questions.stem: A farmer wants her tomato plants to reproduce and produce new seeds. Describe the sequence of events that must happen for new tomato seeds to form.
questions.modelAnswer: First, pollination occurs: pollen from the anther is transferred to the stigma of the flower, usually by insects or wind. Next, fertilisation occurs: the pollen nucleus travels down to the ovary and fuses with the ovule. After fertilisation, the ovule develops into a seed, and the ovary develops into a fruit containing the seed.

questions.stem: When Alexey comes out of a swimming pool on a hot day, he feels cold even though the air is warm. Explain why.
questions.modelAnswer: The water on his skin evaporates, changing from liquid to gas. Evaporation requires heat, and this heat is taken from his body. As his skin loses heat, he feels cold.

questions.stem: Why do some plants have fruits with hooks on the outside?
questions.modelAnswer: The hooks allow the fruits to attach to the fur of passing animals. The animals then carry the fruits away from the parent plant and drop them in a new location, where the seeds can germinate. This is dispersal by animals, and it spreads the seeds so that young plants do not compete with the parent plant for sunlight, water, and nutrients.

questions.stem: On a humid morning, water droplets appear on the outside of a cold drink can. Explain how the water droplets formed.
questions.modelAnswer: Water vapour is present in the surrounding air. When the water vapour touches the cold surface of the can, it loses heat and cools down. The water vapour then condenses, changing from a gas to a liquid, and forms water droplets on the outside of the can.

questions.stem: Describe one similarity and two differences between evaporation and boiling.
questions.modelAnswer: Similarity: both evaporation and boiling are processes in which a liquid changes to a gas. Differences: evaporation takes place only at the surface of the liquid while boiling takes place throughout the liquid. Evaporation can happen at any temperature below the boiling point, while boiling only happens at the boiling point.

misconceptions.correction: A nymph is the juvenile stage in INCOMPLETE metamorphosis and already looks like a small adult. A larva is the second stage in COMPLETE metamorphosis and looks very different.

misconceptions.claim: "A butterfly turns into a chrysalis; the chrysalis is the third stage."
misconceptions.correction: The chrysalis is the casing around the pupa. The PUPA is the third stage. The butterfly forms inside while the pupa undergoes internal reorganisation.
No. Grasshoppers, cockroaches, and dragonflies go through INCOMPLETE metamorphosis with three stages and no pupa.

plant-reproduction.title: Reproduction in Plants
keyQuestions: What are the parts of a flower and their functions?
What is pollination? How does it happen?
What is the difference between wind and insect pollination?
What is fertilisation?
What are the methods of seed dispersal?
What conditions are needed for germination?
keyIdeas: A flowering plant reproduces through four steps: pollination (pollen transferred from anther to stigma), fertilisation (pollen fuses with ovule), seed formation, and seed dispersal.
Seeds are dispersed by wind (light seeds with wings), water (seeds that float), animals (seeds with hooks or inside fruits), or explosive mechanism (pods that burst open).

glossary.definition: Transfer of pollen from anther to stigma. Can be by wind or insects.
glossary.template: The transfer of ___ from the ___ to the ___ of a flower.

glossary.definition: The joining of a pollen nucleus with an ovule inside the ovary to form a seed.
glossary.template: The fusion of ___ with ___ to form ___.

glossary.definition: The sprouting of a seed into a new plant. Needs water, oxygen, and warmth (NOT light).
glossary.template: The process by which ___ develops into ___.

glossary.definition: Male part of the flower. Made up of anther (produces pollen) and filament.
Female part of the flower. Made up of stigma (receives pollen), style, ovary (contains ovules), and ovule.
Green leaf-like parts that protect the flower bud.
Brightly coloured parts that attract insects for pollination.
Spreading of seeds away from the parent plant to reduce competition.
Producing new plants without seeds — e.g. runners (strawberry), bulbs (onion), cuttings.

misconceptions.claim: "Pollination and fertilisation are the same step."
misconceptions.correction: Pollination is the TRANSFER of pollen from anther to stigma. Fertilisation is the LATER fusion of the pollen nucleus with the ovule inside the ovary. Pollination must happen first.
Germination needs water, air (oxygen), and warmth. A seed does NOT need light to germinate. Light is needed for photosynthesis AFTER the shoot breaks the surface.
Wind-pollinated flowers typically have small, dull petals (or none) and feathery stigmas. Brightly coloured petals belong to insect-pollinated flowers.

water-cycle.title: Water Cycle
keyQuestions: What causes water to evaporate?
What is the difference between evaporation and condensation?
How are clouds formed?
What role do plants play in the water cycle?
Why is the water cycle important?
keyIdeas: Matter exists in three states: solid (fixed shape and volume), liquid (fixed volume but takes shape of container), gas (no fixed shape or volume). The particles are arranged and move differently in each state.
Matter changes state through heating (melting, evaporation, boiling) and cooling (freezing, condensation). These changes do not create or destroy matter; only its state changes.
Evaporation happens at any temperature below boiling, at the surface of a liquid. Boiling happens only at the boiling point and throughout the liquid. Both turn liquid into gas.

The water cycle is the continuous movement of water between Earth and the atmosphere: evaporation from oceans, lakes, and rivers; condensation into clouds; precipitation as rain; collection in water bodies.

glossary.definition: The change of state from liquid water to water vapour (gas). Happens at the surface of water when heated by the Sun. Faster with higher temperature, more surface area, or wind.
glossary.template: The process by which ___ changes to ___ at ___.

glossary.definition: The change of state from water vapour (gas) to liquid water. Occurs when warm, moist air rises and cools. This is how clouds and fog form.
glossary.template: The process by which ___ changes to ___ when ___.

glossary.definition: Water falling from clouds as rain, hail, sleet, or snow. Occurs when water droplets in clouds become heavy enough to fall.
The process by which plants release water vapour through tiny pores (stomata) on their leaves. Contributes significantly to the water cycle.
Water that flows over land surfaces into rivers, lakes, and eventually the sea after precipitation.
Water that gathers in oceans, lakes, rivers, and underground aquifers. This is where most evaporation begins.
Water soaking into the ground through soil, eventually reaching underground water stores called aquifers.
Underground layers of rock or sediment that hold large amounts of groundwater, which can feed springs and wells.
Water in its gaseous state. It is invisible and makes up part of the air. When it cools, it condenses into visible water droplets.

misconceptions.claim: "Boiling and evaporation are the same thing."
misconceptions.correction: Boiling happens only at the boiling point and throughout the liquid. Evaporation happens only at the surface and at any temperature below the boiling point. Both turn liquid into gas.

misconceptions.claim: "Condensation happens because the air is wet."
misconceptions.correction: Condensation happens when water vapour in the air COOLS and loses heat, turning it back into liquid water. The trigger is cooling, not wetness.
Clouds are made of tiny LIQUID water droplets (or ice crystals) formed by condensation. Water vapour is a gas and is invisible.

plant-lifecycle.title: Life Cycles of Plants
keyQuestions: What are the main stages of a flowering plant's life cycle?
What conditions does a seed need to germinate?
What happens inside a seed as it grows into a seedling?
How does an adult plant produce new seeds?
Do all plants have the same life cycle?
keyIdeas: A flowering plant's life cycle has four main stages: seed, seedling, young plant, and adult plant. The adult plant produces new seeds, which start the cycle again.
Germination needs water, air (oxygen), and warmth. Light is NOT needed until the seedling emerges above the soil and begins photosynthesis.
A seed contains a tiny embryo plus stored food in the cotyledon. The embryo uses the stored food to grow into a seedling.
Once a seedling has leaves above the soil, it makes its own food by photosynthesis and no longer depends on the stored food in the seed.
The adult plant produces flowers, which are pollinated and fertilised to form fruits and seeds, continuing the cycle.
Non-flowering plants such as ferns and mosses reproduce using spores, not seeds. Their life cycle follows different stages.

glossary.definition: The series of stages a living thing goes through from birth to reproduction and death. The plant life cycle repeats through every generation.
A structure containing a tiny embryo and stored food, protected by a seed coat. Under the right conditions the seed germinates into a new plant.
The tiny undeveloped plant inside a seed. It grows into the seedling once germination begins.
The part of the seed that stores food for the growing embryo. Feeds the seedling until it has leaves and can photosynthesise.
The tough outer layer that protects the seed from drying out and from being damaged until conditions are right for germination.
The process by which a seed begins to grow into a seedling. Needs water, air, and warmth; NOT light.
The very young plant that grows from a germinated seed, using food from the cotyledon until its leaves appear.
The part of the plant that grows downward, anchoring it in the soil and absorbing water and mineral salts.
The part of the young plant that grows upward towards the light, developing leaves, stem, and eventually flowers.
The stage between seedling and adult. Grows leaves, stems, and roots, and makes its own food by photosynthesis.
A mature flowering plant that produces flowers and fruits. The adult stage is when reproduction takes place.
The reproductive part of a flowering plant, containing the stamen (male) and pistil (female). Its pollination and fertilisation lead to new seeds.
The structure that forms from the ovary after fertilisation and protects the seeds. Some fruits also help with seed dispersal.
A tiny reproductive cell used by non-flowering plants such as ferns and mosses instead of seeds.

pslePrompts: When asked about germination, always state the three conditions: water, air, warmth. Examiners penalise answers that add 'light' or 'sunlight'.
Use the terms 'cotyledon' and 'embryo' precisely. The cotyledon is the food store; the embryo is the baby plant.
Flowering plants and non-flowering plants reproduce differently (seeds vs spores). Do not assume all plants work like a mango tree.

misconceptions.correction: Germination needs water, air, and warmth only. Light becomes important AFTER the seedling pushes above the soil and begins photosynthesis.

misconceptions.claim: "Once a seedling has grown, it still gets all its food from the cotyledon."
misconceptions.correction: The cotyledon feeds the seedling only until it has leaves. From then on the young plant makes its own food by photosynthesis in its leaves.

misconceptions.claim: "All plants have the same life cycle."
misconceptions.correction: Flowering plants reproduce using seeds inside fruits. Non-flowering plants like ferns and mosses use spores. Their stages differ.

misconceptions.claim: "The first leaves of the seedling come from the soil."
misconceptions.correction: They come from the cotyledon or grow out of the shoot. Soil provides water and mineral salts; it does not provide leaves or food.

matter.title: Matter: Three States
keyQuestions: What are the three states of matter and how do they differ?
How does heating or cooling change the state of matter?
What is the difference between evaporation and boiling?
Do the particles themselves change during a state change?
Does a gas have mass?
keyIdeas: Matter exists in three states: solid (fixed shape and volume), liquid (fixed volume but takes the shape of its container), and gas (no fixed shape or volume).
The particles are very close and only vibrate in a solid, loosely packed and flow in a liquid, and widely spaced and move freely in a gas.
Heating gives the particles more energy. Solids melt to liquids; liquids evaporate or boil to gases.
Cooling removes energy. Gases condense to liquids; liquids freeze to solids.
Evaporation happens only at the SURFACE of a liquid, at any temperature below boiling. Boiling happens THROUGHOUT the liquid, only at the boiling point.
State changes do not create or destroy matter. The same particles are present; they are just arranged or moving differently. Mass is conserved.
Gases do have mass. Air has mass; a balloon or a bicycle tyre gets heavier when inflated.

glossary.definition: Anything that has mass and takes up space. All substances we can see or feel are made of matter.
A state of matter with a fixed shape and a fixed volume. Particles are close and only vibrate.
glossary.template: A state of matter with a fixed ___ and fixed ___.

glossary.definition: A state of matter with a fixed volume but no fixed shape. It takes the shape of its container. Particles are loosely packed and flow.
A state of matter with no fixed shape and no fixed volume. It fills its container. Particles move freely.
The tiny building block of matter. Particles are always moving; how they move decides the state.
The change of state from solid to liquid when the solid is heated to its melting point.
glossary.template: The change from ___ to ___ when heated.

glossary.definition: The change of state from liquid to solid when the liquid is cooled to its freezing point.
glossary.template: The change from ___ to ___ when cooled.

glossary.definition: The change of state from liquid to gas at the surface of the liquid, at any temperature below boiling point.
The change of state from liquid to gas that happens throughout the liquid at its boiling point.
The change of state from gas to liquid when a gas is cooled.
glossary.template: The change from ___ to ___ when cooled.

glossary.definition: The temperature at which a solid turns into a liquid. Pure ice melts at 0 degrees Celsius.
The temperature at which a liquid boils throughout into a gas. Pure water boils at 100 degrees Celsius.
The amount of space something takes up. Solids and liquids have a fixed volume; gases do not.
Matter is neither created nor destroyed during a state change. The same particles are still there.

pslePrompts: Always state whether a state change is caused by heating or cooling. Examiners want the trigger named, not just the change.
For evaporation vs boiling: surface vs throughout, any temperature vs only at boiling point. Say both differences.
Water vapour is invisible. What you see above a kettle or in a cloud is tiny liquid droplets (condensation), not the gas itself.

misconceptions.correction: It turns into water vapour, which is an invisible gas. The water is still there in the air, and can condense back into liquid when cooled.
Ice, liquid water, and water vapour are all the same substance (H2O) in three different states. Heating ice turns it into water; heating water turns it into vapour.
Gases have mass. A balloon or a bicycle tyre gets heavier when it is inflated. Air pressing down on us is gas with mass.

misconceptions.claim: "Boiling and evaporation are the same thing."
misconceptions.correction: Evaporation happens only at the SURFACE, at ANY temperature below boiling point. Boiling happens THROUGHOUT the liquid, only AT its boiling point.
